input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The case study focuses on the digital twin of an AECO building asset, conceived as a cyber‑physical construct that integrates static Building Information Modelling (BIM) data with runtime sensor and actuator streams. The twin represents the building envelope, HVAC, lighting, and control systems, enabling simulation, live monitoring, and predictive optimisation throughout the building life‑cycle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The twin delivers a suite of services for stakeholders: real‑time monitoring of system states derived from embedded BIM‑based sensor/actuator models; optimisation and predictive analytics through custom DT services; control interfaces that expose building control logic to Facility Managers; and interactive visualization dashboards for immersive inspection and decision support. These services are accessible via the DT Engineer’s plugin framework and consumed by Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and other system operators.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a model‑driven engineering workflow that enriches conventional BIM with BCS (Building Components) models, then applies MDE transformations to produce deployable DT artefacts. Real‑time data exchange synchronises physical and virtual instances, while a modular architecture with standardized extension points supports third‑party services and analytics. The stack includes BIM authoring tools, MDE tooling for model extraction and generation, a data middleware layer for continuous synchronisation, and an interactive visualization component for monitoring and control interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The framework supports continuous, live bidirectional synchronization between the physical building and its digital counterpart. Real‑time data exchange ensures that state changes in the physical system are reflected in the virtual twin with negligible latency. The document does not provide explicit polling intervals, latency limits, or temporal granularity specifications.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The DT constellation follows a modular, extensible pattern. Building Control Engineers create device models in BIM tools and deploy them to the DT Framework. The framework offers standardized extension points, enabling DT Engineers to plug in custom analytics and services. Runtime configuration management allows adjustments without full redeployment. The central hub manages resources, ensures scalability, and maintains system‑wide coherence across the building life cycle.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Horizontal integration is facilitated through the framework’s standardized extension points and bidirectional data exchange interfaces, enabling seamless interoperability with heterogeneous enterprise systems and legacy infrastructures. External systems and other Digital Twins can connect via these interfaces, exchange sensor data, and issue control commands, thereby preserving interoperability and low‑overhead communication across the building ecosystem.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system’s computational foundation is built upon enriched Building Information Model (BIM) files in which sensors, actuators and controllers are explicitly defined; a Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) pipeline extracts these semantic artifacts into structured representations (geometric, behavioral, and analytical models) that are automatically compiled into deployable Digital Twin artefacts; real‑time telemetry is managed through Eclipse Hono for ingestion and Eclipse Ditto for state synchronization, while the enriched BIM photometric metadata (e.g., Revit light power in watts, lux, Kelvin) is parsed and used by the runtime to generate accurate UE5 visualizations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuating devices are instantiated in BIM (e.g., lighting fixtures, HVAC dampers) and exposed as TwinComponents in the UE5 runtime; these components are linked to physical hardware through a DittoController that receives Hono brokered command messages, thereby enabling remote actuation of the underlying devices; safety and operational checks are enforced by the Ditto policy engine which validates command ranges and units before dissemination to the devices.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are modeled directly in BIM (such as photometric LEDs, temperature and humidity probes) and bound to TwinComponents; their measurement streams are harvested via Hono, validated against device metadata (value ranges, units), and routed to Ditto devices for state propagation; the reuse of Revit photometric values enables the runtime to render lighting conditions that mirror the measured scene.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is guaranteed through the MDE transformation’s validation step, where the enriched BIM is checked against the IFC schema and custom rules; runtime data integrity is enforced by Ditto’s policy‑based validation (ensuring sensor readings fall within defined ranges and units) and by Hono’s at‑least‑once message delivery coupled with Ditto’s persistent event storage, which together provide fault tolerance and audit trails for data consistency.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical-to-virtual data flows are enabled through the framework’s real‑time data exchange capability, which continuously synchronizes data between the physical building and its digital twin. The documentation emphasizes continuous real‑time exchange but does not specify the data types, communication protocols, transmission frequencies, event triggers, data quality assurance processes, preprocessing steps, or detailed integration mechanisms. The approach relies on the modular architecture and standardized extension points to allow the ingestion of sensor and operational data into the DT.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control flows from the digital twin to the physical building are facilitated by the same bidirectional data exchange mechanism described in the framework. The documents note that the DT framework acts as a central hub enabling real‑time monitoring and control via facilities managers, but do not detail the specific command types, control message formats, validation, authentication, feedback loops, conflict resolution, or confirmation of command execution. The focus remains on providing an interface for real‑time monitoring and control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The technical networking of the DT framework is described primarily in terms of interoperability across heterogeneous systems and scalability from components to entire infrastructure, but specific communication protocols, network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security protocols are not explicitly defined in the documents. The framework’s modular architecture and standardized extension points imply support for modern connectivity standards, but exact technical specifications are not provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployment details for the DT framework are discussed in terms of runtime configuration management, allowing adjustments without full redeployment, and modular architecture for evolvability. The documents do not specify whether the DT is hosted on cloud, edge, or on‑premise platforms, nor do they provide computational and storage sizing, scalability mechanisms, deployment models, or availability and reliability assurance strategies. The framework is presented as a central hub facilitating bidirectional data exchange for building lifecycle management, but hosting infrastructure specifics are omitted.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
Phase 1 – Device Modeling: Building Control Engineers enrich BIM models with explicit sensor, actuator, and controller elements, creating a unified semantic foundation.
Phase 2 – Model Transformation: An MDE (Model-Driven Engineering) pipeline automatically extracts structured representations from the enriched BIM models.
Phase 3 – Artifact Generation: The system generates deployable Digital Twin artifacts (e.g., micro‑service bundles or container images) with minimal manual intervention.
Phase 4 – Deployment: Building Control Engineers initiate real‑time DT deployment directly from BIM, provisioning the required controllers and data pipelines.
Phase 5 – Operation: Facility Managers continuously monitor system states, interact with the twin, and control building operations via the twin’s real‑time data exchange layer.
Phase 6 – Evolution: DT Engineers extend functionality through custom services and plugins, leveraging standardized extension points in the modular architecture.
Governance: Actor roles defined in Table I (Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer) coordinate responsibility; lifecycle transitions are automatable; a modular architecture ensures version consistency and rollback capability.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The framework follows a model‑driven engineering methodology, using MDE to convert enriched BIM models into deployable DT artifacts. Quality assurance resides in automated transformations that eliminate manual model mapping errors; iterative validation is performed during the deployment phase with real‑time data synchronization to spot inconsistencies. Version control is implied by the use of structured model repositories, although explicit tooling is not detailed. Evolution management is achieved via the framework’s modular architecture and standardized extension points, allowing DT Engineers to add or update custom services (e.g., analytics, AI inference) without perturbing legacy components.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the domain as AECO and the nature of the system as a cyber-physical construct integrating BIM with runtime data, which aligns with the text's definition of DTs in the AECO domain. It correctly mentions capabilities like simulation, monitoring, and optimization. However, the specific physical asset is described in the text as a 'Living Lab', which the extraction generalizes to a 'building asset'. The extraction also lists specific components (envelope, HVAC, lighting) which are reasonable inferences for a building but are not explicitly listed as components of *this specific* twin in the provided snippets. Score reflects accurate general context but missing specific case study detail.

The extraction correctly identifies the main services—monitoring, optimization, control, and visualization—and the target users (Facility Managers, DT Engineers) as defined in the 'Actor Roles' table and the abstract. It accurately describes the plugin framework for extension. The text explicitly supports 'real-time monitoring', 'simulation and life cycle-encompassing optimization', and 'interactive visualization' (Abstract). The score is high because it captures the specific stakeholder roles and service capabilities mentioned in the requirements section.

The extraction provides a comprehensive and accurate list of tools and technologies. It correctly identifies BIM as the semantic backbone, MDE for transformations, Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange, and Unreal Engine for visualization. The text explicitly states: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization', and describes the 'model-driven framework' and 'automated extraction' of data. The extraction is very specific and strongly grounded in the provided evidence.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

This is supported by the text mentioning 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms' and 'automating the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces' to address 'fragmented data sources' and 'heterogeneous infrastructure environments'. The specific claim about 'preserving interoperability and low-overhead communication' is a reasonable inference from the text's focus on interoperability and maintainability requirements.

in the IFC model. Specifically, the LightTwinComponent
component adds a UE5 light to the associated 3D representation and interprets Revit-specific photometric values (provided via device metadata in the BIM model) for accurate

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

The extraction correctly identifies that the documents describe the connection primarily through interoperability and scalability rather than specific network protocols or topologies. It accurately notes that bandwidth, latency, and security protocols are not defined, with the focus remaining on the modular architecture and extension points.

The extraction accurately captures the text's focus on runtime configuration management and the model-driven deployment process. It correctly identifies that the documents omit details regarding hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational sizing, and reliability strategies.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
